summary: Gruber reflects on Jason Kottke's analysis of the NYT editorial board's densely hyperlinked one-paragraph endorsement against Trump, agreeing that it exemplifies the unique power of hypertextual writing. He argues that hypertext links function as an information-density multiplier, adding a third dimension to traditionally two-dimensional prose. Gruber notes that after 20+ years of writing Daring Fireball, hypertext feels like his natural mode of writing. He praises the Times editorial as remarkable precisely because the Times rarely writes in this web-native style. He also laments that social media platforms have largely kneecapped hypertextual writing by only supporting raw URLs rather than inline hyperlinks, calling preview cards a regression in information density.
key_points: Hypertext links are not merely information-density additives like bold or italics — they are information-density multipliers that add a layer of depth even when readers don't follow them
Traditional writing feels two-dimensional while web writing adds a third dimension of depth through hyperlinks, changing how sentences read even passively
The NYT editorial stood out because the Times almost never writes in this web-native hypertextual style, despite being a digital publication
Social media platforms have kneecapped hypertextual writing by limiting users to pasting raw URLs rather than embedding links in prose, and preview cards actually decrease information density
Markdown was designed with the premise that plain text should feel like styled hypertext
money_quotes: Italic and bold emphasis are information-density additives. But as Kottke observes, used deftly, hypertext links are an information-density multiplier.
It's not hypertext writing that now feels like a special form of writing. It's just writing. It's non-hypertext writing that now feels slightly weird to me. Limiting.
What hypertext links provide is an extra layer of depth. Just the fact that the links are there — even if you, the reader, don't follow them — makes a sentence read slightly differently.

Reading this 110-word/27-link firecracker of an exhortation to end the Trump era wasn't just pleasing to my reading ear, it was like hearing a beautiful song sung by a voice that I can only recall heretofore having spoken. I didn't know they could sing, let alone sing like that.
It's more like texting in public than writing for the real web.
If anything, turning links into preview cards significantly decreases information density. That feels like a regression, not progress.
Traditional writing — like for print — feels two-dimensional. Writing for the web adds a third dimension.
key_insight: Hypertext links are an information-density multiplier unique to the web, adding depth to prose even when readers never click them, yet social media platforms have largely abandoned this power in favor of raw URLs and preview cards.
